content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 907 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

April 29, 1977

John Zierold
1107 Ninth Street, Suite 419
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Mr. Zierold:

This letter is in response to my telephone conversation with
Katie Blewett of your office, on April 21, 1977, regarding your
duties and responsibilities as a Lobbyist registered under the
Political Reform Act.

First, you have asked if you may purchase tickets to a fund-
raising dinner hosted by the controlled committee of an elected
state officer. Sections 86200 and 86202 of the Political Reform
Act provide that it shall be unlawful for a lobbyist to make a
contribution to a state candidate, a committee supporting a state
candidate or an elected state officer. Section 82015 states in
part:..."Contribution" includes the purchase of tickets for events
such as dinners, luncheons, rallies and similar fund raising
events..." Since the inception of the Act, the Commission has
stated that the full price of a ticket to a fundraiser is a
contribution. Therefore, it would be unlawful for you to purchase
tickets to a fundraising dinner hosted by the controlled committee
of an elected state officer.

Second, you have asked if your wife may purchase tickets to
a fundraising dinner hosted by the controlled committee of an
elected state officer. The provisions of the Political Reform
Act are not applicable to the spouse of a Lobbyist, unless the
activities of the spouse are such that he or she would be subject
to the provisions of the Act in his or her own right. However,
it should be noted that Sections 86200 and 86202 provide that, in
addition to being unlawful for a Lobbyist to make a contribution,
it shall be unlawful for a Lobbyist to act as an agent or inter-
mediary in the making of a contribution to those persons or
by himself or any other person. Therefore, your wife may purchase
tickets to a fundraising dinner hosted by the controlled committee
of an elected state officer, so long as she is acting entirely
independently of you and for purposes unrelated to your lobbying
activities.

TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE ADMINISTRATION (916) 322-5660 • EXECUTIVE/LEGAL 322-5901 • ENFORCEMENT/CONFLICT OF INTEREST 322-6441

Third, you have asked if you may attend a fundraising dinner
hosted by the controlled committee of an elected state officer,
if someone else purchased the tickets and has asked you to go.
The Political Reform Act does not restrict you from attending
fundraising dinners hosted by controlled committees of elected
state officers. However, as previously noted you may not make a
contribution by purchasing tickets, act as an agent or intermedia-
ry in the making of a contribution or arrange for the making
of a contribution by yourself or any other person.

This letter deals only with your questions regarding fund-
raising dinners hosted by controlled committees of elected state
officers and does not address the issue of purchasing tickets to
events such as testimonial dinners, where none of the proceeds
are used for political purposes.

If I can be of further assistance, do not hesitate to contact
me.

Sincerely,

Alan Herndon
Compliance Representative

AH:bw

bcc: Ted Prim

sections.question: 1) May a registered lobbyist purchase tickets to a fundraising dinner hosted by the controlled committee of an elected state officer? 2) May a lobbyist's spouse purchase such tickets? 3) May a lobbyist attend such a dinner if someone else purchased the tickets?
sections.conclusion: A lobbyist may not purchase tickets to a fundraising dinner hosted by a controlled committee of an elected state officer, as ticket purchases constitute contributions prohibited by Sections 85200 and 86202. A lobbyist's spouse may purchase tickets if acting entirely independently and for purposes unrelated to the lobbyist's lobbying activities. A lobbyist may attend a fundraising dinner if someone else purchased the tickets, but may not make contributions, act as an agent/intermediary, or arrange contributions.
sections.question_synthetic: The document addresses three related questions about whether a registered lobbyist and their spouse may purchase or attend fundraising dinner tickets hosted by controlled committees of elected state officers.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Registered lobbyists are prohibited from purchasing fundraising dinner tickets as this constitutes a prohibited contribution. Their spouses may purchase tickets if acting independently, and lobbyists may attend dinners purchased by others, but cannot facilitate or arrange contributions in any capacity.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document lacks traditional 'QUESTION' and 'CONCLUSION' section headers but content clearly follows this structure. Minor OCR errors present ('Callfortya', 'hobbyist' instead of 'lobbyist') but do not impede comprehension. Letter dated April 29, 1977.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1) May a registered lobbyist purchase tickets to a fundraising dinner hosted by the controlled committee of an elected state officer? 2) May a lobbyist's spouse purchase such tickets? 3) May a lobbyist attend such a dinner if someone else purchased the tickets?

CONCLUSION: A lobbyist may not purchase tickets to a fundraising dinner hosted by a controlled committee of an elected state officer, as ticket purchases constitute contributions prohibited by Sections 85200 and 86202. A lobbyist's spouse may purchase tickets if acting entirely independently and for purposes unrelated to the lobbyist's lobbying activities. A lobbyist may attend a fundraising dinner if someone else purchased the tickets, but may not make contributions, act as an agent/intermediary, or arrange contributions.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter responding to inquiries by a registered lobbyist regarding the legality of purchasing or attending fundraising dinner tickets hosted by controlled committees of elected state officers under the Political Reform Act.
